name: European-Commission-Prompt-Impact
description: Horizon Europe Impact section (Section 2: Expected Outcomes, Dissemination, Exploitation)

variants.name: default
variants.text:
You are writing SECTION 2: IMPACT of a Horizon Europe grant proposal (Part B - Technical Description). INPUTS: - {{content}}: The researcher's actual work, publications, and technical expertise. This is your PRIMARY source for understanding what the research produces, who benefits from it, and what real-world change it enables. - {{grant_info}}: The specific Horizon Europe call and topic description, including Topic ID, Expected Outcomes, Expected Impacts, and any specific requirements. YOUR TASK: Generate ONLY Section 2: Impact. Do NOT generate Section 1 (Excellence), Section 3 (Implementation), work packages, budgets, or any other proposal component. Those are handled separately. EVALUATION CRITERION: The extent to which the project's outputs will contribute to the expected outcomes and impacts described in the work programme, and the quality of the measures for dissemination, exploitation, and communication. SECTION STRUCTURE: 2.1 EXPECTED OUTCOMES AND IMPACTS (approximately 6 pages) (a) Contribution to Expected Outcomes: - For each Expected Outcome listed in {{grant_info}}, explain specifically how the results from {{content}} contribute to it - Use the exact language from the work programme topic when referencing expected outcomes - Ground every contribution claim in what {{content}} actually produces — do NOT invent outputs not described in {{content}} - Provide concrete, measurable indicators of achievement based on the work described in {{content}} - Describe the pathway: how do the specific results from {{content}} get taken up and used by target groups? Results to Outcomes Pathway: - Describe the logical chain from {{content}}: Project Activities (what {{content}} describes) → Outputs/Results → Outcomes → Impacts - Identify key assumptions and dependencies for this pathway to work - Be realistic about what {{content}} can deliver within a project timeframe (b) Contribution to Destination Impacts and EU Policy Objectives: - Explain how the research described in {{content}} contributes to the broader impacts in the work programme Destination from {{grant_info}} - Reference relevant EU strategies from {{grant_info}} only where {{content}} genuinely connects (e.g., Green Deal, Digital Strategy, Cancer Mission) - Describe the long-term vision: what changes in society, economy, or science does the work in {{content}} ultimately enable? - Address relevant UN SDGs only if the connection to {{content}} is direct and credible Societal and Economic Impacts: - Describe expected societal benefits based on what {{content}} produces and for whom - Quantify economic impacts only where {{content}} provides supporting data or evidence - Describe environmental impacts if the research in {{content}} has environmental relevance Scientific and Technological Impacts: - Describe contributions to scientific knowledge that follow directly from {{content}} - Explain technological advances enabled by the work in {{content}} - Describe how results from {{content}} will influence future research directions Impact Timeline: - Short-term (during project): what {{content}}-based outputs will be available? - Medium-term (1-5 years after): what uptake or adoption is realistic given the maturity described in {{content}}? - Long-term (5+ years): what systemic change does the work in {{content}} contribute to? Barriers and Risks to Impact: - Identify key external barriers to achieving the impacts described above - Describe measures to overcome these barriers - Note: focus on external barriers beyond project scope — internal implementation risks belong in Section 3 2.2 MEASURES FOR DISSEMINATION, EXPLOITATION, AND COMMUNICATION (approximately 4 pages) Overall Strategy: - Describe the overall approach to maximizing use and impact of the results from {{content}} - Explain how dissemination, exploitation, and communication are integrated - Note that a detailed Dissemination and Exploitation Plan will be submitted as a deliverable by month 6 Dissemination of Results: Target Audiences: - Identify primary scientific/technical audiences for the results from {{content}} - Identify secondary audiences (practitioners, policymakers, industry) relevant to {{content}}'s domain Dissemination Activities: - Peer-reviewed publications: describe publication strategy for results from {{content}}, target journals/conferences, open access commitment - Conference presentations: list key conferences relevant to the field of {{content}} - Workshops and seminars: describe knowledge exchange activities - Open access: commit to open access for all peer-reviewed publications; describe data repositories relevant to the data types in {{content}} Exploitation of Results: Exploitable Results: - List the key exploitable results that emerge from {{content}} (technologies, methods, datasets, software, guidelines, policy recommendations) - For each result: describe its nature, potential applications, target markets or users, and exploitation pathway - Ground this list in what {{content}} actually produces — do NOT invent exploitable results Exploitation Pathways: - Commercial: products, services, licensing, spin-offs where supported by {{content}} - Non-commercial: policy uptake, standards, guidelines, public services where relevant to {{content}} - Further research: follow-on projects, scale-up, replication Intellectual Property: - Describe approach to IP protection appropriate to the results from {{content}} - Address open source vs. proprietary decisions based on the nature of {{content}}'s outputs Market Analysis (for IA): - If {{grant_info}} indicates an Innovation Action, describe the target market for results from {{content}} - Describe the competitive landscape and go-to-market strategy based on what {{content}} produces Communication Activities: Public Communication Strategy: - Describe approach to communicating the work in {{content}} to general public and media - Project website: launch timeline, content strategy - Social media: platforms and content approach relevant to {{content}}'s audience - Press releases: key milestones from {{content}} that warrant public communication - Policy briefs: if {{content}} has policy-relevant findings, describe how these will be communicated to decision-makers WRITING GUIDELINES: - Write for evaluators who will check every impact claim against the research described in {{content}} - Be specific about who benefits and how — generic statements about societal benefit without grounding in {{content}} will weaken the proposal - Be realistic about impact timelines given the maturity of the work in {{content}} - Do NOT fabricate market sizes, adoption rates, or impact metrics not supported by {{content}} - Do NOT include URLs to external websites - Define all acronyms on first use ALIGNMENT: - Read {{grant_info}} carefully — directly reference each Expected Outcome from the topic description - If {{grant_info}} indicates an IA (Innovation Action), give more weight to exploitation and market analysis - If {{grant_info}} indicates an RIA (Research and Innovation Action), give more weight to scientific dissemination and knowledge generation OUTPUT FORMAT: Generate well-structured HTML: - Use

for the section title Section 2: Impact - Use

for subsections (2.1, 2.2) - Use

for sub-subsections - Use

for paragraph content - Use

    /
  • for lists - Use for emphasis sparingly - Target approximately 10 pages (5,000-6,500 words)
